title: Classical ML Algorithms (KNN, SVM, Trees)
blurb: ★★★ Core / must-know · Foundational classifiers taught in the data-driven approach: K-Nearest Neighbors (non-parametric), linear classifiers with Multiclass SVM (hinge) and Softmax losses, and Decision Trees and Random Forests based on information gain and ensembling.

knowledge_points.title: ⭐ Mock Exam: KNN
knowledge_points.recap: KNN is a favorite concept question: how it is 'trained' (lazy — just store data), how it is tested (distance → K nearest → majority vote), and how to choose K (validation/cross-validation + bias-variance trade-off).

questions.q:
(a) How is the **K-Nearest Neighbor** algorithm trained?
(b) How is it tested?
(c) How would you identify the right number of neighbors **K**?
questions.answer:
**(a) Training:** KNN is a **lazy learner** — there is essentially **no training phase**; 'training' just **stores** the training feature vectors and their labels.

**(b) Testing** a new point: compute its **distance** (e.g. Euclidean) to all training points, find the **K nearest** neighbors, then **majority vote** over their labels (optionally distance-weighted); for regression, average the neighbors' targets.

**(c) Choosing K:** use a **validation set or cross-validation** — try $K=1,2,\dots,20$, measure validation performance, pick the best. Trade-off: **small K** → low bias, high variance (noise-sensitive); **large K** → smoother boundary, higher bias, may underfit.
questions.extend: Memory: KNN 'trains' by memorizing; test = distance→K nearest→vote; pick K by CV (bias-variance).

knowledge_points.title: K-Nearest Neighbors & Hyperparameter Search
knowledge_points.recap: KNN is a non-parametric, memory-based classifier that predicts a test point's label by majority vote among its K nearest training points. K and the distance metric are hyperparameters chosen via a validation set or cross-validation.

questions.q: Explain how the K-Nearest Neighbors classifier works and why it is called non-parametric.
questions.answer: KNN **memorizes all training data and labels**. To classify a test point, it finds the **K closest training points** (by a distance metric) and takes a **majority vote** of their labels. It is **non-parametric** because it makes no assumption about the underlying data distribution and learns no parameters/weights: there is essentially no training step; all computation happens at prediction time. With $K=1$ it copies the label of the single nearest neighbor; larger $K$ smooths decision boundaries.
questions.extend: Memorize, then majority-vote the K nearest.

questions.q: Give the formulas for the L1 (Manhattan) and L2 (Euclidean) distances used in KNN, and name the two hyperparameters.
questions.answer: For points $p$ and $q$: **L1 (Manhattan)** $d_1(p,q) = \sum_i |p_i - q_i|$; **L2 (Euclidean)** $d_2(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_i (p_i - q_i)^2}$. The two **hyperparameters** are the value of **K** and the choice of **distance metric**. Both are problem-dependent and are set (not learned) using a validation set.
questions.extend: L1 = sum of |diffs|; L2 = sqrt of sum of squares.

questions.q: Why should hyperparameters (like K) be chosen on a validation set rather than on the training or test set?
questions.answer: Choosing on the **training set** is bad because it always favors the model that memorizes (e.g. $K=1$ gives perfect training accuracy). Choosing on the **test set** is bad because you then have no unbiased estimate of performance on new data. A separate **validation set** (or cross-validation) is used to tune hyperparameters, and the **test set is used only once at the very end**.

options.text: Tuning on training data always favors trivial memorization; tuning on test data destroys the unbiased final estimate
The validation set is always larger than the training set
Hyperparameters cannot be evaluated on training data at all
The test set must be used repeatedly during tuning

questions.extend: Touch the test set exactly once.

questions.q: Explain K-Folds cross-validation and two drawbacks of KNN on images (curse of dimensionality).
questions.answer: **K-Folds cross-validation** splits the training data into K folds; each fold serves once as the validation set while the rest train the model, and results are **averaged**. It is useful for **small datasets**. KNN on images is never used because: (1) it is **very slow at test time** (must compare against all stored points); (2) **distance metrics on raw pixels are not informative** (boxed/shifted/tinted images can have identical L2 distance); and (3) the **curse of dimensionality** — the number of points needed to densely cover the space grows exponentially with dimension.
questions.extend: High dimensions need exponentially more data.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to standardize features, fit a KNN classifier and evaluate it (a rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, accuracy_score, classification_report

# 1. Train/test split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    df.drop('TARGET CLASS', axis=1), df['TARGET CLASS'], test_size=0.30)

# 2. Standardize (fit on train only, then transform both)
scaler = StandardScaler()
scaler.fit(X_train)
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test = scaler.transform(X_test)

# 3. Fit KNN
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=1)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)

# 4. Predict and evaluate
pred = knn.predict(X_test)
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, pred))
print('Test accuracy:', accuracy_score(y_test, pred))
print(classification_report(y_test, pred))
```
**StandardScaler** removes the mean and scales to unit variance — important for distance-based methods like KNN. The scaler is fit on the training set only to avoid data leakage.
questions.extend: Scale before KNN; fit scaler on train only.

variants.code:
```python
scaler = ____(1)()
scaler.fit(X_train)
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)
knn = ____(2)(n_neighbors=1)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```python
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=____(1))
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
pred = knn.____(2)(X_test)
print(accuracy_score(y_test, pred))
```

```python
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = ____(1)(
    df.drop('TARGET CLASS', axis=1), df['TARGET CLASS'], test_size=0.30)
scaler = StandardScaler()
scaler.____(2)(X_train)          # fit on TRAIN only (no leakage)
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
```

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to pick the best K for KNN by tracking the error rate (a rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier

error_rate = []
for i in range(1, 40):
    knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=i)
    knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
    pred_i = knn.predict(X_test)
    error_rate.append(np.mean(pred_i != y_test))

# Best K = index of minimum error (+1 because range starts at 1)
K = np.argmin(error_rate) + 1
print('Best K =', K)

# Retrain with the best K
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=K)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
pred = knn.predict(X_test)
```
Plotting error rate vs. K (the 'elbow' method) reveals the K that best balances bias and variance.
questions.extend: argmin(error) + 1 gives best K.

variants.code:
```python
error_rate = []
for i in range(1, 40):
    knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=____(1))
    knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
    pred_i = knn.predict(X_test)
    error_rate.append(np.____(2)(pred_i != y_test))
```

```python
K = np.____(1)(error_rate) + ____(2)   # +offset: range starts at 1
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=K)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```python
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=K)
knn.____(1)(X_train, y_train)
pred = knn.____(2)(X_test)
```

knowledge_points.title: Linear Classifiers: SVM (Hinge) & Softmax Loss
knowledge_points.recap: A linear classifier computes scores $f(x,W) = Wx + b$. A loss function quantifies how bad the scores are: Multiclass SVM uses hinge loss (margin-based), Softmax uses cross-entropy on normalized probabilities. Regularization prevents overfitting.

questions.q: Describe the parametric linear classifier $f(x,W) = Wx + b$ and the three viewpoints for interpreting it.
questions.answer: A **linear classifier** stretches the input image into a column vector $x$ and computes class scores $f(x,W) = Wx + b$, where $W$ is the weight matrix and $b$ the bias. For CIFAR-10, $x \in \mathbb{R}^{3072}$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{10\times 3072}$, giving 10 class scores. Three viewpoints: (1) **Algebraic** — a matrix multiply $Wx+b$; (2) **Visual** — each row of $W$ is a learned **template** per class; (3) **Geometric** — the classifier draws **hyperplanes cutting up the space** into class regions.
questions.extend: One row of W = one class template = one hyperplane.

questions.q: Write the Multiclass SVM (hinge) loss and explain the role of the margin.
questions.answer: For an example with scores $s = f(x_i, W)$ and correct label $y_i$, the **Multiclass SVM loss** is $$L_i = \sum_{j \ne y_i} \max(0,\; s_j - s_{y_i} + 1).$$ It is a **hinge loss**: it incurs zero cost as long as the correct-class score exceeds every wrong-class score by at least the **margin** of 1; otherwise it charges the shortfall linearly. The full-dataset loss is the average $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i L_i$. Minimum loss is 0 (all margins satisfied); maximum is unbounded.
questions.extend: Hinge = max(0, wrong - correct + margin).

questions.q: Given scores cat=3.2, car=5.1, frog=-1.7 for an image whose correct class is 'cat', compute the Multiclass SVM loss (margin = 1).
questions.answer: The correct score is $s_{y_i} = 3.2$. Sum the hinge over the other classes: $$L = \max(0, 5.1 - 3.2 + 1) + \max(0, -1.7 - 3.2 + 1)$$ $$= \max(0, 2.9) + \max(0, -3.9) = 2.9 + 0 = 2.9.$$
questions.extend: Only violated margins add to the loss.

questions.q: What is the purpose of adding a regularization term (e.g. L2) to the loss function?
questions.answer: The total loss is **data loss + $\lambda\cdot$ regularization**, where $\lambda$ is the regularization strength. Regularization **prevents the model from doing too well on the training data** (overfitting): it expresses a preference for simpler weights, helps the model generalize to test data, and can improve optimization by adding curvature. Note that the SVM loss alone is not enough to pick a unique $W$ — if $L=0$ for some $W$, then $2W$ also gives $L=0$; regularization breaks this ambiguity.

options.text: To prevent overfitting by preferring simpler weights and improving generalization
To make the training accuracy as high as possible
To guarantee the loss reaches exactly zero
To remove the need for a validation set

questions.extend: L2 reg: simple weights generalize better.

questions.q: Contrast the Softmax classifier with the Multiclass SVM. How does Softmax turn scores into a loss?
questions.answer: The **Softmax** classifier (multinomial logistic regression) interprets scores as **unnormalized log-probabilities (logits)**. It exponentiates and normalizes: $P(y=k\,|\,x) = \frac{e^{s_k}}{\sum_j e^{s_j}}$ so probabilities are $\ge 0$ and sum to 1. The loss is the **cross-entropy** $L_i = -\log P(y_i\,|\,x_i)$. Key difference: the **SVM (hinge) loss saturates** — once margins are satisfied it stops caring about the exact scores — whereas **Softmax always wants to push the correct probability toward 1**, so it keeps improving scores even when classification is already correct.
questions.extend: SVM stops at the margin; Softmax never fully satisfied.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to fit an SVM and tune C/gamma with GridSearchCV (a rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix

# Baseline SVM
svc_model = SVC()
svc_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
predictions = svc_model.predict(X_test)

# Tune hyperparameters with grid search
param_grid = {'C': [0.1, 1, 10, 100], 'gamma': [1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001]}
grid = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True, verbose=1)
grid.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(grid.best_estimator_)

grid_pred = grid.predict(X_test)
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, grid_pred))
print(classification_report(y_test, grid_pred))
```
**C** controls the soft-margin penalty (trading training error for margin width / stability); **gamma** is the RBF-kernel width for non-linear boundaries. The `kernel` can also be tuned (`'linear'`, `'poly'`, `'rbf'`, `'sigmoid'`).
questions.extend: C = soft-margin penalty; gamma = RBF width.

variants.code:
```python
svc_model = ____(1)()
svc_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
param_grid = {'C': [0.1, 1, 10, 100], 'gamma': [1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001]}
grid = ____(2)(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True, verbose=1)
grid.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```python
param_grid = {'____(1)': [0.1, 1, 10, 100], '____(2)': [1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001]}
grid = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True, verbose=1)
grid.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(grid.____(3))
```

```python
grid = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True, verbose=1)
grid.____(1)(X_train, y_train)
grid_pred = grid.____(2)(X_test)
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, grid_pred))
```

knowledge_points.title: Decision Trees & Random Forests
knowledge_points.recap: Decision trees split data at nodes chosen to maximize information gain (entropy reduction). Random Forests reduce variance by ensembling many randomized trees and voting/averaging their predictions.

questions.q: How does a decision tree choose which attribute to split on? Define entropy and information gain.
questions.answer: For a training set with $p$ positive and $n$ negative examples, the **entropy** is $$H\!\left(\tfrac{p}{p+n}, \tfrac{n}{p+n}\right) = -\tfrac{p}{p+n}\log_2\tfrac{p}{p+n} - \tfrac{n}{p+n}\log_2\tfrac{n}{p+n}.$$ An attribute $A$ with $K$ values splits $E$ into subsets $E_1,\dots,E_K$; the **expected remaining entropy** is $EH(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{K}\frac{p_i+n_i}{p+n}H\!\left(\frac{p_i}{p_i+n_i}, \frac{n_i}{p_i+n_i}\right)$. The **information gain** is the reduction in entropy $I(A) = H(\ldots) - EH(A)$. The tree **chooses the attribute with the largest information gain**.
questions.extend: Split on the attribute that reduces entropy most.

questions.q: For a training set with p = n = 6 (12 examples), the entropy is H(6/12, 6/12) = 1 bit. An attribute 'Patrons' splits it into groups with entropies giving expected entropy EH = 0.459 bits. What is the information gain?
questions.answer: Information gain is $I = H - EH = 1 - 0.459 \approx 0.541$ bits. (In the lecture example $I(\text{Patrons}) \approx 0.541$ bits, whereas $I(\text{Type}) = 0$ bits, so 'Patrons' is the far better split.) A gain near the root entropy means the attribute is highly informative; a gain of 0 means the split does not reduce uncertainty at all.
questions.extend: Gain = parent entropy minus weighted child entropy.

questions.q: How does a Random Forest improve over a single decision tree?
questions.answer: A **Random Forest** builds an **ensemble** of many randomized decision trees and outputs the **majority vote** (classification) or **mean/average** (regression) of the individual trees. Randomization (bootstrap samples of the data and random attribute subsets per split) makes the trees diverse, so averaging them **reduces variance** without much increase in bias — directly addressing the tendency of single deep trees to overfit.

options.text: It ensembles many randomized trees and votes/averages them, reducing variance
It builds one very deep tree to eliminate bias
It replaces entropy with the Euclidean distance
It removes the need for training data

questions.extend: Many diverse trees + voting = lower variance.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to train a Decision Tree and a Random Forest, then evaluate (a rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, accuracy_score

# Single decision tree
dtree = DecisionTreeClassifier()
dtree.fit(X_train, y_train)
dtree_pred = dtree.predict(X_test)
print('Tree accuracy:', accuracy_score(y_test, dtree_pred))

# Random forest (ensemble of 600 trees)
rfc = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=600)
rfc.fit(X_train, y_train)
rfc_pred = rfc.predict(X_test)
print('Forest accuracy:', accuracy_score(y_test, rfc_pred))
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, rfc_pred))
print(classification_report(y_test, rfc_pred))
```
`n_estimators` sets the number of trees; more trees generally lower variance at higher compute cost. `max_depth` and `n_estimators` are commonly tuned with GridSearchCV.
questions.extend: n_estimators = number of trees in the forest.

variants.code:
```python
dtree = ____(1)()
dtree.fit(X_train, y_train)
rfc = ____(2)(n_estimators=600)
rfc.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```python
rfc = RandomForestClassifier(____(1)=600)
rfc.fit(X_train, y_train)
rfc_pred = rfc.____(2)(X_test)
print(accuracy_score(y_test, rfc_pred))
```

```python
dtree = DecisionTreeClassifier()
dtree.____(1)(X_train, y_train)
dtree_pred = dtree.predict(X_test)
print(____(2)(y_test, dtree_pred))
```

questions.q: A single decision tree gets 100% training accuracy but low test accuracy. Explain the cause and why a Random Forest helps, in bias-variance terms.
questions.answer: A fully grown tree can perfectly memorize the training set, giving **low bias but very high variance** — small changes in the data produce very different trees, so it **overfits** and generalizes poorly. A **Random Forest** trains many trees on bootstrapped samples with random feature subsets and averages/votes their outputs. Averaging many **decorrelated high-variance** models **reduces variance** while keeping bias low, improving test accuracy. This mirrors the bias-variance tradeoff: match model complexity to data, and use ensembling to control variance.
questions.extend: Deep tree = high variance; forest averages it away.

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style questions in the professor's format — concept-first, plus a Keras code item where relevant. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q: (a) How would you **choose the value of K** in K-Nearest Neighbors? (b) What happens to the decision boundary and the bias-variance behaviour as K goes from very small to very large? (c) Would you use KNN for high-resolution image classification? Explain.
questions.answer:
**(a)** Treat **K** as a hyperparameter: try $K=1,2,\dots$ and pick the value with the best **validation-set** (or **cross-validation**) performance. Never tune K on the training set (it always favors $K=1$) or on the test set (it destroys the unbiased final estimate).

**(b)** **Small K** (e.g. $K=1$): a jagged boundary that follows individual points — **low bias, high variance**, very sensitive to noise. **Large K**: a smoother boundary that averages many neighbors — **higher bias, lower variance**; too large and it underfits (predicts the majority class).

**(c)** **No.** KNN is **slow at test time** (compares against all stored points), **L2 distance on raw pixels is uninformative** (shifted/tinted images can be equidistant), and it suffers the **curse of dimensionality** — densely covering a high-dimensional space needs exponentially many examples.
questions.extend: Pick K by CV; small K = high variance, large K = high bias; KNN unsuitable for raw images (slow, bad metric, curse of dimensionality).

questions.q: (a) What benefit does adding a **regularization term** give a linear classifier? (b) Contrast the **Multiclass SVM (hinge)** loss with the **Softmax** loss — how does each treat an example that is *already* correctly classified with a large margin?
questions.answer:
**(a)** The total loss is **data loss $+\ \lambda\cdot$ regularization**. Regularization expresses a preference for **simpler (smaller) weights**, which **reduces overfitting** and improves generalization. It also resolves the SVM's scale ambiguity: if $W$ gives loss 0 then so does $2W$; the L2 penalty breaks this tie and picks a unique, well-behaved $W$.

**(b)** The **hinge (SVM) loss saturates**: once the correct-class score beats every wrong class by the margin (1), the loss is exactly 0 and the classifier **stops caring** about that example. The **Softmax / cross-entropy** loss is $-\log P(y_i\mid x_i)$ with $P=\frac{e^{s_k}}{\sum_j e^{s_j}}$; it is **never fully satisfied** — it keeps pushing the correct-class probability toward 1, so it continues to adjust scores even for already-correct examples.
questions.extend: L2 reg ⇒ simpler weights, better generalization, unique $W$. SVM stops at the margin; Softmax always wants $P\to 1$.

questions.q: (a) How does a **decision tree** decide which attribute to split on? (b) A single fully-grown tree reaches 100% training accuracy but poor test accuracy — explain in **bias-variance** terms. (c) How does a **Random Forest** address this, and why does the randomization matter?
questions.answer:
**(a)** It picks the attribute with the largest **information gain** $I(A)=H - EH(A)$: the parent **entropy** minus the weighted average entropy of the children. The attribute that reduces uncertainty the most is chosen at each node (gain 0 means the split is useless).

**(b)** A fully-grown tree can **memorize** the training set — **low bias, very high variance**. Small changes in the data yield very different trees, so it overfits and generalizes poorly.

**(c)** A **Random Forest** builds many trees on **bootstrap samples** with **random attribute subsets** per split, then **votes (classification)** or **averages (regression)**. The randomization makes the trees **decorrelated**; averaging many diverse high-variance models **reduces variance** while keeping bias low, so test accuracy improves.
questions.extend: Split on max information gain; deep tree = high variance/overfit; forest = decorrelated trees averaged ⇒ variance down.
